## Streamline AI video creation with Dreamina Seedance 2.0 prompts in Dreamina
### Key features of Dreamina's Seedance 2.0 AI video generator
* **Create viral videos from just one sentence:** With Dreamina's Seedance 2.0, one sentence is all it takes to generate a polished 1080p video that matches your vision. Whether it's mirroring viral trends, reinventing classic moments, or producing content inspired by hit videos, AI ensures accuracy and saves you hours of manual editing.
* **Multimodal references for clearer creative direction:** Dreamina Seedance 2.0 supports images (up to 9), video clips (up to 3, each up to 15 seconds), audio tracks (up to 3, each up to 15 seconds), and text references within a single project, enabling richer multimodal guidance for video generation.
* **Consistent character and style learning:** Reference materials help Dreamina Seedance 2.0 accurately capture character appearance, actions, and overall visual identity. This ensures characters remain consistent across scenes, even when camera angles, effects, or environments change.

results.title: How to Use Timeline Prompting with Seedance 2.0 for Cinematic AI Video | MindStudio
excerpts:
# How to Use Timeline Prompting with Seedance 2.0 for Cinematic AI Video
## What Seedance 2.0 Brings to AI Video Generation
Seedance 2.0 is ByteDance’s video generation model built for high-quality, motion-coherent output. Unlike earlier generation models that struggled to maintain subject consistency or translate camera instructions into visible motion, Seedance 2.0 handles both notably well.
Key capabilities relevant to timeline prompting:
* **Temporal coherence** — Subjects remain visually consistent as scenes progress through a clip
* **Camera simulation** — The model responds to cinematographic movement instructions like dolly, pan, tilt, and tracking
* **Scene progression** — It can handle a described sequence of events within a single generation
* **Cinematic vocabulary** — Film-grammar terms like depth of field, rack focus, and motivated camera movement register meaningfully

...

## The Anatomy of a Seedance 2.0 Timeline Prompt
### Camera Movement
Name the specific movement if one occurs:
* **Dolly in/out** — Camera physically moves toward or away from subject
* **Pan left/right** — Camera rotates on vertical axis
* **Tilt up/down** — Camera rotates on horizontal axis
* **Tracking shot** — Camera moves alongside a moving subject
* **Crane/jib up** — Camera ascends for height reveal
* **Handheld** — Slight organic shake for realism
* **Rack focus** — Focus shifts between subjects or planes

...

## Camera Direction Vocabulary Reference
This is a quick reference for camera and cinematography terms that translate reliably in Seedance 2.0 prompts. Use these exactly as written — the model responds to standard film vocabulary.
**Movement terms:**
| Term | Effect |
| Slow dolly in | Gradual approach, builds intimacy or tension |
| Quick pan left/right | Fast reorientation, suggests action or urgency |
| Tracking shot (left to right) | Follows lateral movement |
| Tilt down | Reveals below-frame content |
| Pull back reveal | Starts close, widens to show context |
| Arc shot (orbit) | Camera circles the subject |
| Steadicam walk | Smooth forward movement following a character |

...

## Frequently Asked Questions
### How do I write camera directions that Seedance 2.0 understands?
Use standard cinematography vocabulary: dolly in, dolly out, pan left/right, tilt up/down, tracking shot, rack focus, crane up, arc shot. Seedance 2.0 is trained on video content with associated descriptions, so film-grammar terms are generally understood. Avoid vague language like “the camera moves around the subject” — the more specific the instruction, the more likely the output reflects it.

results.title: Seedance 2.0 Prompt Guide 2026: 30+ Copy-Paste Prompts & Camera Moves | Seedance
excerpts:
# Seedance 2.0 Prompt Guide 2026: 30+ Copy-Paste Prompts & Camera Moves
> **May 2026 Update:** Many searchers ask for an “official” Seedance 2.0 prompt guide. This is an official-style practical field guide, not a ByteDance specification: it gives a repeatable structure, camera-move language, product prompts, image-to-video rules, troubleshooting, and copy-paste templates you can test in Text to Video or Image to Video .

...

## The Core Prompt Structure
Seedance 2.0 responds best to prompts that are structured, not just descriptive. The most reliable format:
```
[Camera/shot type], [Subject], [Action/motion], [Environment], [Lighting], [Visual style/mood]
```
**Example:**
```
Slow dolly push forward, a woman in a red coat walking through a misty autumn forest, leaves falling around her, golden diffused morning light, cinematic color grade, shallow depth of field
```
Compare to a weak version:
```
Woman walking in a forest with leaves falling
```
The structured version generates on the first try significantly more often. The vague version often produces muddy lighting, undefined motion, and inconsistent visual quality.

...

## How Do You Write Prompts for Seedance Text-to-Video?
For text-to-video prompts, follow this structure:
* **Subject:** [person/object/animal] + [description]
* **Action:** [specific movement or activity]
* **Setting:** [location + time of day + weather]
* **Style:** [cinematic/realistic/animated] + [camera movement]
Bad prompt: _"A woman walking in a city"_ Good prompt: _"A young businesswoman in a blue suit walking purposefully through midtown Manhattan at dusk, close-up tracking shot, golden hour lighting, light rain"_

...

## Frequently Asked Questions About Seedance Prompts
**Q: Is this the official Seedance 2.0 prompt guide?** A: This is an official-style practical guide for Seedance users, not a ByteDance spec sheet. It is designed to translate the prompt patterns that work in real workflows into reusable templates, examples, and troubleshooting steps.
**Q: What is the best prompt for Seedance 2.0 video generation?** A: The best starting prompt is: `Shot type, subject, one visible action, environment, camera movement, lighting, visual style, aspect ratio.` For example: `Close-up product shot, white ceramic mug on a walnut desk, steam rising slowly, morning apartment kitchen, subtle dolly forward, soft golden window light, realistic cinematic style, 16:9.`

Dreamina Seedance 2.0 series prompt guide
Dreamina Seedance 2.0 series (hereinafter referred to as Seedance 2.0 series) models are professional-grade video generation models that natively support joint audio and video generation, featuring outstanding semantic understanding and multimodal interaction capabilities. This document introduces the methods and related techniques for using Seedance 2.0 series prompts, helping you efficiently leverage the model to generate high-quality video works that meet your requirements. ​
Note ​
All visual and auditory materials (including images, videos, and audio) presented in this guide are autonomously generated by Se edance/ S eedream visual generation models. ​
01 General principles ​
1.1 Basic formula for text instructions ​
Seedance 2.0 series excels at following natural language logic . You can flexibly combine the following elements based on your creative needs: ​
​ ​ ​
The logical foundation of your generation. Clearly define "who" is performing "what action". ​
​ ​ ​
Define the overall tone by describing the spatial background, lighting details, or specific visual styles. ​

Advanced instructions can include scene or ambient sound effects to achieve an immersive, synchronized audiovisual output. ​ ​
1.2 Reference control for multimodal inputs ​

02 Text in Video 2.1 Slogan / Title Text 2.2 Subtitles 2.3 Speech Bubbles

04 Video Reference 4.1 Action Reference 4.2 Camera Movement Reference 4.3 Effects Reference

## 01 General Tips
### 1.1 Basic Prompt Formula
Seedance 2.0 deeply follows natural language logic, so you can flexibly combine the following elements based on your needs.
Required

#### Subject
The logical foundation of your prompt — clearly define WHO is performing WHAT action.
Required

#### Motion
The logical foundation of your prompt — clearly define WHO is performing WHAT action.
Optional

...

#### Camera
Use camera choreography or ambient sound effects for immersive audiovisual output.
Optional

#### Audio
Use camera choreography or ambient sound effects for immersive audiovisual output.

...

### 1.2 Multimodal Reference Control
#### Clearly Specify References
In your prompt, clearly specify what to reference — e.g., "use the composition from Image 1" or "follow the action from Video 2".

...

## 02 Text in Video
Seedance 2.0 supports generating text overlays in T2V (text-to-video), I2V (image-to-video), R2V (reference-to-video), and V2V (video-to-video) scenarios. The model can automatically match appropriate styles and colors based on context, and also supports specifying text color, style, appearance method, timing, and position in your prompt. Use common characters and avoid rare characters or special symbols for best results.

...

## 04 Video Reference
### 4.2 Camera Movement Reference
Reference [ Video N ]'s [Camera Movement Description], generate [Scene Description], maintaining consistent camera movement.

excerpts:
# Seedance 2.0 API Guide: ByteDance AI Video Model and Prompt Examples
## Seedance Prompt Examples
### **Example 1: Cinematic Environment Scene**
**Seedance 2.0 Output**
Prompt: _A cinematic aerial shot of a coastal city at sunrise. Soft golden light reflects off the ocean while waves crash against a curved shoreline. Small fishing boats move slowly across the water as the camera glides forward above the harbor. The atmosphere feels calm and peaceful with warm morning light._
Seedance 2.0 produces a stable aerial scene with smooth forward camera motion. The lighting transitions and ocean reflections remain consistent across frames, while the movement of boats and waves maintains realistic motion dynamics.

...

### **Example 3: Dynamic Action Scene**
**Seedance 2.0 Output**
Prompt: _A futuristic motorcycle speeding through a neon-lit city at night. Rain falls onto the street while reflections of neon signs glow on the wet pavement. The camera tracks behind the rider as the bike accelerates through narrow streets, creating a fast-paced cinematic chase scene._
Seedance 2.0 demonstrates strong motion stability in a fast-moving environment. The reflections on the wet road and neon lighting remain visually consistent while the camera tracking maintains a smooth cinematic chase perspective.

### **Example 4: I2V Generation**
Reference Image Reference Image
**Seedance 2.0 Output**
Prompt: _A woman sings and strums her guitar on a small stage, warm lighting, cinematic composition._
Seedance 2.0 successfully animates the subject while preserving the identity and stage environment from the reference image. The guitar strumming and body movement appear natural, and the warm stage lighting remains consistent throughout the clip.

### **Example 5: R2V Generation**
**Seedance 2.0 Output**
The R2V generation follows the video extension workflow described in the [API documentation](https://piapi.ai/docs/seedance-api/seedance-2-preview?via=seedance-2-0-api-guide) .
Seedance 2.0 extends the original video by an additional **5 seconds** while maintaining scene continuity and consistent camera motion. Environmental lighting and landscape details remain stable, producing a natural continuation of the original footage.

# Seedance 2.0 Complete Prompt Guide
## Prompt Guide
01 01 Highlights 1.1 Basic Formula 1.2 Multi-modal Control 02 Text Gen 03 Image Ref 04 Video Ref 05 Video Edit 06 Best Practices
01

## 01 Highlights
### 1.1 Basic Formula
Seedance 2.0 deeply follows natural language logic, allowing you to flexibly combine the following elements based on your needs.
Subject
Describe a clear action performer and specific behavior — this is the logical foundation of your prompt.
Environment & Aesthetics
Describe spatial backgrounds, lighting details, or specific visual styles to define the overall tone of the scene.
Camera & Sound
Use advanced directives like camera movement or ambient sound effects to achieve immersive audiovisual output.

...

### 1.2 Multi-modal Control
#### 🎯 Explicit Reference
Clearly specify reference objects in your prompt, e.g. "reference the composition of `Image 1` " or "reference the action from `Video 2` ".

...

## Image Ref
### 3.1 Multi-view Reference
#### ✨ Prompt Template
Copy
Reference/Extract/Combine + "Subject" from "Image n", generate "scene description", keep "Subject" features consistent.

...

#### 📝 Prompt
Try This
Extract the camera from `@Image 1` , `@Image 2` , `@Image 3` , change the background to white. The camera is on a white table, the lens focuses on the camera in close-up, then slowly rotates around it, clearly showing the front, side, and back.

...

Try This
Warm-toned home scene background, mid-shot presenting the thermos from the reference images, camera smoothly pushes in to a close-up of the thermos, a hand enters from off-screen to naturally grip and lift the thermos, camera follows the hand's slight rotation to showcase it.

...

## Video Ref
### 4.1 Action Reference
#### ✨ Prompt Template
Copy
Reference "action description" from "Video n", generate "scene description", keep action details consistent.

...

### 4.2 Camera Reference
#### ✨ Prompt Template
Copy
Reference "camera movement" from "Video n", generate "scene description", keep camera movement consistent.

...

#### 📝 Prompt
Try This
Reference the camera movement from `@Video 1` , create a concept video of a tech park with the high-rise from `@Image 1` as the visual center, also using a first-person diving perspective, highlighting the tech feel of the park in `@Image 1` .

...

## Best Practices
### Core Principles
#### 💡 Universal Starter Formula
[Scene type] + [Subject 1][State 1] + [Subject 2][State 2] + [Environment] + [Lighting & Mood] + [Camera Movement]

results.title: Seedance 2.0 Prompt Engineering: The Exact Structure That Gets Consistent Results - CrePal Content Center
excerpts:
# Seedance 2.0 Prompt Engineering: The Exact Structure That Gets Consistent Results
## The Prompt Anatomy That Works (Subject / Motion / Style / Camera)
I stopped writing prompts as paragraphs and started writing them as four clean blocks. Think of it like giving a film crew a one-page brief.
* Subject: Who/what, wardrobe/props, setting, time, mood
* Motion: Subject action + pace + duration + loop intent
* Style: One core aesthetic + lighting + color grade
* Camera: Framing + lens + movement + restraints (start/end if needed)
I also keep a quiet line for “negatives” (things to avoid) when the output leans cheesy.
Here’s the **skeleton** I actually paste into **Seedance 2.0** :
> _Subject: [clear identity, wardrobe, setting, time, mood]_
> _Motion: [action + pace + duration]_
> _Style: [one primary aesthetic + lighting + color]_
> _Camera: [framing + lens + movement + constraints]_
> _Negatives: [what to avoid]_

### Annotated Prompt Examples, Good vs Bad
Bad (mushy):
“A woman in a kitchen making coffee, cinematic.”
* Why it fails: no wardrobe, no time-of-day, no pace, no camera plan. “Cinematic” is doing all the work, and failing.
Good (holds):
Subject: 30s woman in a minimalist kitchen, oatmeal sweater, steam from kettle, dawn light, calm focus
Motion: lifts pour-over kettle, slow clockwise pour, gentle steam drift for 4 seconds
Style: cinematic natural light, soft contrast, warm grade, filmic grain subtle
Camera: medium close-up at counter height, 50mm look, locked tripod, micro push-in 3%
Negatives: no logo mugs, no cheesy smile, no speed ramp
* Result: On my run (timestamp 00:03–00:07 of the clip), the steam held shape, the hands stayed consistent, and the push-in didn’t wobble.
Pro tip: If your subject identity keeps morphing, bump the subject block to the very first line and add a consistent anchor like “same person across frames.” Seedance 2.0 seems to weight early tokens heavier.

...

## Camera Movement Prompts That Actually Hold
* “locked tripod, micro push-in 2%”
* “gimbal-stable, slow pan left, 3 seconds, stop on center”
* “24mm wide, low angle, track right at constant pace”
* “top-down, product centered, no parallax, no roll”
If your move keeps drifting, add a brace: “no horizon tilt,” “no reframing,” or “keep background fixed.” I sometimes include an end state: “lands on label,” “holds hands center frame,” which cut the last-frame jitter in two of my morning tests.
